  • Hide All Code

Supplemental Online Material (SOM): Empirical Results Based on Cross-Cohort Analyses

Dr Dimitris I. Tsomokos (University College London)

14 April 2025

This document includes details about the cohort-specific operationalisation, weighting strategy, longitudinal models, moderation analyses, sensitivity analyses, and inferential limits for the cross-cohort study (across MCS and ABCD datasets) testing whether motivational engagement is a more informative prospective signal than executive control / cognitive self-efficacy for adolescent mental health outcomes.

1 Overview

1.1 Research question

This supplement tests an indirect prediction from the companion theory in this project (see Notebooks folder in the same Github repo). The starting point is that mental health and the transition into psychopathology is a dynamical process. In the case of children and adolescents, for instance, behaviour changes which parts of the social and school environment are sampled (i.e., explored, engaged with), and the observations drawn from these sampled parts of the environment update beliefs about the world (i.e., internal models, cognitive schemata etc.), and finally those updated beliefs shape the next behavioural choice. This is a feedback loop that, under worsening conditions, can shift from stable engagement to self-reinforcing withdrawal.

The project’s Notebooks translate that idea into a specific empirical claim. Notebook 01 shows that a minimal active-inference system can generate abrupt shifts between behavioural states. Notebook 02 applies that result to adolescent psychopathology and predicts that transitions into and out of maladaptive states should depend more on the motivational value of engagement than on decisional precision alone. Notebook 03 adds a second prediction: balance measures, such as log-ratios of distress to wellbeing or motivation, may sometimes index transition-relevant position (in the phase diagram), although they cannot separate the underlying components. See more about these notebooks below.

Therefore, the central empirical question is: after accounting for baseline mental health problems and standard covariates, are later internalising and disengagement-related outcomes predicted more strongly by earlier motivation or engagement than by earlier executive-control competencies?

Throughout the supplement, motivation/engagement is used as the observational counterpart of the theoretical preference field: how worthwhile participation in ordinary life still feels. Executive-control is used as the observational counterpart of policy precision: how consistently action can be selected and carried through once engagement is attempted. (NOTE! These analyses do not estimate the latent active-inference quantities directly. They test whether the broad longitudinal ordering seen in MCS and ABCD is more consistent with a field-dominated than a precision-dominated developmental architecture.)

1.2 Theoretical companion notebooks

This supplement sits downstream of three theoretical notebooks in the Series: Quantifying Phase Transitions in Psychopathology.

  • Notebook 01 derives the formal result: a minimal active-inference model with learning-action coupling can show phase-transition-like shifts between behavioural states.

  • Notebook 02 gives that result a clinical interpretation in adolescent psychopathology and makes the main prediction used here, namely that recovery-relevant trajectories should be organised more strongly by the motivational value of engagement (\(\Delta c\)) than by decisional precision or self-efficacy alone (\(\gamma\)).

  • Notebook 03 then derives the balance-index prediction: log-ratio measures may track a transition-relevant phase-space coordinate, but they recover only a product term and therefore cannot identify the underlying components separately.

The role of the present supplement is not to recover those latent parameters from cohort data. It is to test whether the broad longitudinal patterns in MCS and ABCD are compatible with the empirical predictions generated by those notebooks.

1.3 Hypotheses

  1. Better motivation and engagement should predict lower later distress, less disengagement, and lower likelihood of persistence-related psychopathology outcomes.
  2. Motivation/engagement predictors should outperform executive-control predictors in the main longitudinal models, because the theory predicts that the value of re-engagement is the more central organising process.
  3. Family, peer, school, sleep, and deprivation-related variables should modify these pathways, showing where the main developmental signal is amplified or buffered by context.
  4. Genetic scores are tested as potential vulnerability modifiers.
  5. Balance indices such as \(\psi = \log(\text{distress} / \text{wellbeing-or-motivation})\) may capture transition-relevant position efficiently, but that advantage is treated as empirical, outcome-specific, and not assumed in advance.

1.4 Variable strategy

The supplement uses cohort-specific operationalisation rather than forcing artificial one-to-one harmonisation. Each cohort contributes the variables it actually contains. Motivation/engagement measures were chosen to reflect reward responsiveness, school or social investment, and future-oriented participation where possible. Executive-control measures were chosen to reflect decision quality, working memory, inhibitory control, cognitive flexibility, and parent- or self-reported control problems where possible.

Overview of the main construct families used across MCS and ABCD.

1.5 Inferential scope and limits

!These analyses can support broad claims about developmental pathways into distress, persistence, withdrawal, and disengagement. They cannot directly estimate latent active-inference parameters, recover formal cusp geometry, establish mathematical early-warning signals, or prove path-asymmetric hysteresis!

These limits are crucial to bear in mind because the imported theoretical language can otherwise sound stronger than the data warrant. The present analyses are indirect tests of developmental architecture. They ask whether ordinary cohort measures line up better with one broad ordering of processes than another. They do not show that any observed variable is itself Δc, γ, a posterior belief, or a memory-state parameter.

The most defensible claim, therefore, is deliberately narrow: if motivation/engagement measures show larger and more consistent prospective associations than executive-control measures, and if the coefficient difference is itself statistically reliable, then the cohort data are more compatible with the theory’s central developmental emphasis. That is the main message this supplement is designed to test.

1.6 Summary

Section 1 frames the study in ordinary clinical-scientific language. The central comparison is not “motivation versus control” in an all-or-none sense, but whether motivational disengagement is the more central prospective signal. Any support for the theory depends on that direct ordering test and remains indirect and observational.

2 Preliminary Data Analysis Pipeline

2.1 Data sources and analytical logic

The two cohorts are used differently but with a common logic.

  • MCS spans ages 11, 14, 17, and 23 in the harmonised descriptive pipeline, drawing on parent-reported emotional or peer problems, adolescent school and engagement measures, decision-making indicators, sleep and family context, standalone distress and wellbeing measures in later sweeps, and local genetic scores (Polygenic Risk Scores, or PRS). The main inferential regressions are anchored at age 14, with the balance-index analyses using age 17 as the exposure wave.
  • ABCD contributes repeated childhood-to-adolescent waves, with sample flow shown across all available sessions. The main ABCD analyses stop at age 15 (ses-05A) to preserve a larger analytic sample; age 16 (ses-06A) can be reserved for sensitivity analyses (since in Release 6.1 currently the last wave is partial and has < half of its cohort members).

For MCS, inferential models are survey-weighted using the weight from the exposure wave. In the local longitudinal family file, FOVWT2 is the whole-UK weight for age-14 exposure models and GOVWT2 is the whole-UK weight for age-17 exposure models. The same file also provides the stratum variable PTTYPE2 and the fieldwork-point cluster variable SPTN00. This means that age-14 to age-17 models and age-14 to age-23 models can both be estimated with the appropriate age-14 survey design, while any future age-17 to age-23 models should use the age-17 weight.

The pipeline proceeds in four stages:

  1. first we build cohort-specific descriptive and longitudinal samples;
  2. we derive motivation/engagement, executive-control, context, and outcome variables;
  3. we fit prospective models that compare motivation/engagement against executive-control;
  4. we test whether context and, in MCS only, genetic liability modify those pathways.

The central lagged ABCD comparison therefore uses ses-02A to ses-05A, which yields 6,417 complete predictive cases.

For MCS, the motivation/engagement composite was assembled from school enjoyment, schoolwork enjoyment, being happy with friendships, effort and interest in school, and perceived school value because these indicators map most closely onto the theory’s field-like construct: the subjective value of remaining engaged with ordinary developmental tasks and social participation. In this framework, the field parameter is not intended to capture all positive functioning; it is intended to capture whether everyday participation feels rewarding enough to sustain approach rather than withdrawal. This is why the composite prioritises school and friendship items, which directly index the affective and motivational pull of routine engagement, over other activity items available in MCS, such as going to the cinema or watching live sport, which are more contingent on opportunity, transport, finances, and family organisation, and over Rosenberg-style self-esteem items, which are conceptually closer to global self-evaluation than to the immediate value of behavioural engagement.

The main limitation is that the MCS composite is weighted toward school-linked motivation and does not exhaust the wider motivational field available to adolescents. It also contains some positive wellbeing content, so later wellbeing outcomes (only used in secondary analyses) should be interpreted as more construct-adjacent than the main later-distress analyses. We therefore treat it as a theory-led proxy rather than a definitive measurement model of \(`\Delta c`\), and future work could usefully test broader alternative composites as a sensitivity analysis rather than replacing these items post hoc.


Internal-consistency estimates for the MCS composite scales used in the main age-14 analyses. Alpha is standardised Cronbach’s alpha and omega is McDonald’s omega total, both computed on the oriented item sets used to construct the composites.
Composite Wave Participants with item data Complete-case N Items (k) Cronbach’s alpha Omega
Motivation/engagement composite age14 11711 11118 6 0.763 0.768
Executive-control primary proxy age14 11711 10705 1 NA NA

These reliability estimates support the motivation/engagement composite as a moderately coherent proxy scale. The primary MCS executive-control variable is a single quality-of-decision-making indicator, so internal-consistency coefficients are not applicable to that main proxy; delay aversion and word activity score are retained as alternative single-indicator sensitivity operationalisations.

For MCS, cohort-member files were restricted to cohort member 1 so that only singletons and first-born children were retained consistently across sweeps; in the PRS file the equivalent restriction was PNUM = 100. The local PRS variables are available only for the White-European genetic subset released in the CLS genomics repository.

For ABCD, the primary motivation/engagement composite uses school environment, school involvement, and reverse-scored school disengagement because this school-participation cluster showed the clearest and most coherent fit to the theory’s field-like construct at the chosen baseline session. BAS indicators remain available in the dataset and are retained for secondary analyses such as the balance-index work, but they were not retained in the final primary field composite. (As in MCS, this is a theory-led proxy rather than an exhaustive measure of positive functioning, and its emphasis is on approach valuation and participation rather than on general wellbeing.)

Internal-consistency estimates for the ABCD composite scales used at the baseline session of the main lagged analyses. Alpha is standardised Cronbach’s alpha and omega is McDonald’s omega total, both computed on the oriented item sets used to construct the composites.
Composite Wave Participants with item data Complete-case N Items (k) Cronbach’s alpha Omega
Motivation/engagement composite ses-02A 10898 10865 3 0.749 0.778
Executive-control primary proxy ses-02A 10898 8050 1 NA NA

For ABCD, the primary executive-control variable is the ses-02A NIH Toolbox Flanker score. Internal-consistency coefficients are not applicable to that single-indicator proxy, and the broader multi-item baseline executive-control set was not retained because reverse_bdefs_total is unavailable at ses-02A and overlap among the other candidate NIH Toolbox indicators is too sparse for a defensible composite.

2.4 Model comparison framework

The main prospective test is nested. In each cohort we fit:

  • a baseline model with prior distress and core covariates;
  • a baseline-plus-motivation/engagement model;
  • a baseline-plus-executive-control model;
  • a combined model containing both predictor families.

If motivation/engagement truly carries the larger developmental signal, it should improve model fit more consistently, retain the larger combined-model coefficient, and that coefficient should be significantly larger than the executive-control coefficient in a direct within-model comparison.

Model comparison metrics for the main lagged analyses, including model N and missingness handling.
cohort model N Missingness aic bic delta_aic
ABCD Base model 7296 Complete-case analysis -3467.74 17432.48 31.39
ABCD Motivation/engagement model 7282 Complete-case analysis -3499.13 17368.20 0.00
ABCD Executive-control model 5680 Complete-case analysis -2598.30 13715.54 900.83
ABCD Combined model 5674 Complete-case analysis -2631.68 13671.75 867.45
MCS Base model 8949 Complete-case analysis 24813.68 8472.51 1967.85
MCS Motivation/engagement model 8864 Complete-case analysis 24149.35 8008.81 1303.53
MCS Executive-control model 8395 Complete-case analysis 23289.74 7970.25 443.92
MCS Combined model 8371 Complete-case analysis 22845.83 7608.43 0.00

2.5 Summary

Section 2 describes the analytic flow. MCS spans ages 11 to 23 descriptively within one harmonised framework, but the inferential regressions are anchored on age-14 exposures except for the age-17 balance-index models; those inferential models use exposure-wave survey weights. ABCD sample flow is shown across all available sessions, but the main inferential analyses stop at age 15 so the core comparison is estimated on the larger ses-02A to ses-05A sample.

3 Millennium Cohort Study

3.1 MCS sample and measurement focus

The MCS analyses use age 14 as the main predictive baseline because that sweep gives the strongest local combination of symptom, engagement, decision-making, and context measures. It includes parent-reported emotional and peer problems, school and social satisfaction items, school effort and interest, Cambridge Gambling Task indicators, a word-activity score, sleep latency, family conflict, peer-related trouble, and local genetic scores.

Ages 11 and 14 use the SDQ internalising composite (emotion + peer problems), age 17 uses the standalone Kessler-6 psychological-distress measure (GDCKESSL), and age 23 uses the standalone Kessler-6 psychological-distress measure (HDKESSLER6). Age 17 wellbeing (GDWEMWBS) and the age 23 anxiety, depression, loneliness, and wellbeing measures are retained as secondary/additional outcomes.

The primary MCS inferential models use the age-14 survey design because age 14 is the exposure wave. Concretely, the weighted design uses FOVWT2 for whole-UK weighting, PTTYPE2 for stratum, and SPTN00 for fieldwork-point clustering. The same age-14 design is carried forward when age-14 predictors are used to model age-17 and age-23 outcomes. This is the correct design logic for these exposure-based models.

All primary MCS regression models use age-14 predictors, age-14 covariates, and either age-17 or age-23 outcomes. The standard adjustment set is age-14 baseline distress where the model is prospective, plus age-14 age, sex, PTTYPE2 sampling stratum, and the age-14 OECD equivalised income (FOECDUK0 given in quintiles). In other words, the predictors and covariates come from the same exposure wave.

In terms of the analytic sample, only cohort member 1 was retained in the cohort-member files, which corresponds to keeping singletons and first-born children (among twins and triplets) in a family. In the CLS PGI file, the equivalent first cohort-member is PNUM = 100 so we use that to combine the dataframes. Those PRS variables are available only for the White-European genetic subset released by CLS, so all MCS genetic analyses are necessarily run in that subset.

Demographic and socioeconomic profile of the primary MCS analytic sample used in the complete-case age-14 to age-17 comparison models.

3.2 MCS trajectories

The figure below is descriptive only and uses the main wave-specific distress backbone rescaled to a pooled z metric so that broad age-related shifts can be visualised. It should be read as a longitudinal overview of the main distress outcome, not as proof of strict measurement invariance across instruments, or indeed an actual average trajectory for this cohort. It is also descriptive rather than design-weighted.

MCS descriptive trajectory of the main distress outcome across ages 11, 14, 17, and 23, shown on a pooled-standardised scale.

MCS wave-level descriptive summary for the main distress outcome shown on a pooled-standardised scale.
wave cohort n_rows n_participants outcome_mean outcome_sd outcome_missing
age11 MCS 13277 13277 -0.46 0.67 3.8%
age14 MCS 11711 11711 -0.35 0.72 3.3%
age17 MCS 10831 10831 0.39 1.04 7.8%
age23 MCS 9560 9560 0.65 1.11 3.4%

3.3 Motivation/engagement versus executive-control comparison

This primary MCS comparison uses age-14 motivation/engagement and executive-control predictors, an age-17 psychological-distress outcome, and age-14 covariates. The covariates entered are age-14 baseline distress, age-14 age, age-14 sex, age-14 PTTYPE2 sampling stratum, and age-14 OECD equivalised income; the survey design uses the age-14 weight, stratum, and cluster variables.

The fitted model is a survey-weighted Gaussian svyglm on a standardised age-17 distress outcome. Because the substantive continuous predictors and covariates are z-scored at the exposure wave, B and Std. b are numerically identical for those terms; the intercept has no standardised value and is therefore shown as a dash in the full table.

MCS survey-weighted combined-model coefficients for the motivation/engagement and executive-control predictor families, using age-14 exposure-wave weights and age-17 distress as the outcome.

MCS survey-weighted combined lagged model comparing motivation/engagement and executive-control predictors from age 14 to age 17, with model N and missingness handling shown explicitly.
predictor_family estimate std_error statistic p_value N Missingness
Motivation/engagement -0.302 0.023 -12.983 < .001 8371 Complete-case analysis
Executive-control primary proxy 0.021 0.014 1.464 .144 8371 Complete-case analysis
Direct coefficient-comparison test for the MCS combined model. Negative values indicate that the motivation/engagement coefficient is more negative than the executive-control coefficient.
outcome estimate_difference std_error statistic p_value N Missingness
Age 17 psychological distress -0.323 0.028 -11.665 < .001 8371 Complete-case analysis

In the survey-weighted combined MCS model, the motivation/engagement composite showed a much larger prospective association with later distress than the executive-control composite. The key point is that this is not only visible descriptively in the coefficient sizes; it is supported by a direct coefficient-comparison test within the same combined model. This is the clearest single result in the study because it speaks directly to the theory’s strongest empirical implication in a form that ordinary cohort data can test.

3.3.1 Full model results

The full combined MCS model below shows all coefficients entered into the primary survey-weighted regression. Predictors and covariates are from age 14, the outcome is age-17 psychological distress, and the design uses the age-14 survey weight, stratum, and cluster variables.

Full survey-weighted MCS combined model: age-14 predictors and covariates to age-17 psychological distress.
Outcome Model N Missingness Term B 95% CI Std. b Statistic p
Age 17 psychological distress Combined model 8371 Complete-case analysis Intercept 0.026 [-0.017, 0.068] — 1.186 .236
Age 17 psychological distress Combined model 8371 Complete-case analysis Baseline distress 0.160 [0.132, 0.188] 0.160 11.331 < .001

Age 17 psychological distress Combined model 8371 Complete-case analysis PTTYPE2 stratum: Northern Ireland - Disadvantaged -0.074 [-0.194, 0.047] -0.074 -1.198 .231
Age 17 psychological distress Combined model 8371 Complete-case analysis Motivation/engagement composite -0.302 [-0.348, -0.257] -0.302 -12.983 < .001
Age 17 psychological distress Combined model 8371 Complete-case analysis Executive-control primary proxy 0.021 [-0.007, 0.049] 0.021 1.464 .144

3.4 MCS precision-proxy operationalisation sensitivity

The primary MCS executive-control proxy is age-14 quality of decision making from the Cambridge Gambling Task. To check whether the main ordering depends narrowly on that choice, the same weighted age-14 to age-17 combined model was re-estimated after replacing the primary precision proxy with delay aversion and then with the age-14 word-activity score.

MCS precision-proxy operationalisation sensitivity analyses for the primary age-14 to age-17 weighted model.
outcome Alternative precision proxy Field estimate Precision estimate Coefficient-comparison p N Missingness status
Age 17 psychological distress Delay aversion -0.300 0.014 < .001 8368 Complete-case analysis Estimated
Age 17 psychological distress Word activity score -0.307 0.110 < .001 8423 Complete-case analysis Estimated

3.5 Missing-data sensitivity: MI

As a missing-data sensitivity analysis for the primary MCS finding, the same combined age-14 to age-17 model was re-estimated after multiple imputation using m = 20 imputations. (The imputation object is cached in outputs/derived/mcs_primary_mi_m20.rds). Imputation was applied to the analysis variables under a MAR assumption, and each completed dataset was analysed with the same survey-weighted svyglm, with coefficients then pooled using Rubin’s rules.

MCS missing-data sensitivity analysis for the primary combined model using multiple imputation (m = 20) and pooled survey-weighted estimates.
predictor_family estimate std_error statistic p_value N Missingness
Motivation/engagement -0.305 0.023 -13.158 < .001 9856 Multiple imputation (m = 20), pooled with Rubin’s rules
Executive-control primary proxy 0.016 0.015 1.074 .283 9856 Multiple imputation (m = 20), pooled with Rubin’s rules
Direct coefficient-comparison test for the MCS multiple-imputation sensitivity model.
outcome estimate_difference std_error statistic p_value N Missingness
Age 17 psychological distress -0.322 0.029 -11.245 < .001 9856 Multiple imputation (m = 20), pooled with Rubin’s rules

The missing-data sensitivity preserves the same substantive question as the complete-case model, but replaces listwise deletion with multiple imputation. This provides a more defensible check that the main MCS ordering is not an artefact of complete-case restriction alone.

3.6 MCS context moderation

These moderation models retain the same age-14 predictor wave and the same age-14 adjustment set as the main prospective model. Each moderation analysis therefore asks whether an age-14 contextual exposure changes the strength of the age-14 motivation/engagement or executive-control association with age-17 distress, after adjustment for age-14 baseline distress, age, sex, PTTYPE2 sampling stratum, and income quintile.

Each moderation model is a survey-weighted Gaussian svyglm with age-17 distress as the standardised outcome and age-14 z-scored predictors, moderators, and covariates entered at the exposure wave.

MCS survey-weighted context moderation results (age 14 to age 17).
model term estimate std_error statistic p_value N Missingness
Family conflict moderation Motivation/engagement composite x family conflict 0.010 0.025 0.402 .688 8264 Complete-case analysis
Family conflict moderation family conflict x Executive-control primary proxy -0.004 0.020 -0.221 .825 8264 Complete-case analysis
Sleep moderation Motivation/engagement composite x sleep-risk composite 0.043 0.020 2.103 .036 8314 Complete-case analysis
Sleep moderation sleep-risk composite x Executive-control primary proxy 0.047 0.016 3.008 .003 8314 Complete-case analysis
Peer-school moderation Motivation/engagement composite x peer-related school trouble -0.061 0.024 -2.527 .012 7248 Complete-case analysis
Peer-school moderation peer-related school trouble x Executive-control primary proxy -0.018 0.017 -1.102 .271 7248 Complete-case analysis

These moderators were chosen because the theory does not imply that motivation unfolds in a social vacuum. Family conflict, peer-related difficulty, and sleep problems are plausible background conditions that can either amplify or buffer the consequences of low motivation and disengagement. Sleep latency is especially useful here because it is both clinically interpretable and modifiable: if the motivation/distress association is stronger under poorer sleep initiation, that supports the more modest theoretical claim that so-called orthogonal influences may modulate the main pathway rather than replace it. In other words, sleep would matter because it changes how strongly low engagement translates into later problems.

The most credible MCS context effects in this pass involve family conflict, sleep, and peer-related school trouble. That pattern suggests that the motivation/engagement pathway is not simply an individual trait pathway, but one shaped by interpersonal strain, physiological regulation, and social difficulty.

3.6.1 Full model results

Each full moderation model below uses age-14 predictors, an age-14 contextual moderator, the age-14 covariate set, and the age-14 survey design, with age-17 psychological distress as the outcome.

3.7 MCS genetic-score moderation

This section retains a deliberately focused pair of exploratory moderation models. Depression PRS is included because it is the most clinically proximate inherited-liability score for the present framework, and cognition PRS is included as the closest available genetic analogue of the project’s precision or executive-control family. Both models use age-14 behavioural predictors, age-17 distress as the outcome, the same age-14 sociodemographic adjustment set as above, and the age-14 survey design.

The fitted models are survey-weighted Gaussian svyglm interaction models. Age-14 behavioural predictors, age-14 covariates, and ancestry PCs are z-scored before entry, so B and Std. b are directly comparable for the substantive coefficients shown below.

Exploratory survey-weighted MCS genetic-score moderation results (age 14 to age 17).
model term estimate std_error statistic p_value N Missingness
Depression PRS moderation Motivation/engagement composite x depression PRS (reverse-scored) -0.018 0.031 -0.581 .562 4940 Complete-case analysis
Depression PRS moderation depression PRS (reverse-scored) x Executive-control primary proxy -0.002 0.017 -0.130 .897 4940 Complete-case analysis
Cognition PGI moderation Motivation/engagement composite x cognition PGI 0.006 0.028 0.224 .823 4940 Complete-case analysis
Cognition PGI moderation cognition PGI x Executive-control primary proxy -0.028 0.018 -1.569 .118 4940 Complete-case analysis

These genetic analyses are available only for the White-European genetic subset in the CLS release and, in line with the cohort-member handling above, use the first cohort member only (CNUM00 = 1 in sweep files; PNUM = 100 in the PRS file).

These genetic-score analyses remain exploratory. Their role is not to stand in for latent active-inference parameters, but to test whether inherited liability or a precision-adjacent cognitive PRS shifts vulnerability to the motivational or contextual pathways identified above. In the present focused set, depression PRS remains the more clinically relevant moderator and cognition PRS serves as the expected comparison from the executive-control side, but neither model provides clear interaction evidence The weighting improves the representational validity of the age-17 endpoint, but it does not remove the broader interpretive limits of PRS analyses.

3.7.1 Full model results

Each full genetic-score moderation model below uses age-14 behavioural predictors and age-14 covariates, adds age-14 survey design variables through the weighted analysis, and includes ancestry principal components pc1 to pc20. The outcome is age-17 psychological distress.

3.8 MCS sensitivity analyses with separate later outcomes

The separate wellbeing, anxiety, depression, and loneliness measures are modelled here as distinct alternative outcomes rather than being absorbed into a single omnibus score. This matters because it checks whether the main ordering result is specific to broad distress only or generalises across conceptually related later outcomes. The age-17 and age-23 sensitivity models keep the same age-14 predictor wave and the same age-14 covariate set as the primary MCS analysis. The added age-14 MFQ model is different: it is a same-wave sensitivity analysis of age-14 depressive symptoms, adjusted for age-14 age, sex, PTTYPE2 sampling stratum, and income quintile, and is therefore descriptive of baseline association rather than prospective prediction.

Survey-weighted MCS sensitivity analyses using separate alternative outcomes. Coefficient-comparison p values test whether the motivation/engagement coefficient differs from the executive-control coefficient within the same combined model.
outcome predictor_family estimate std_error statistic p_value N Missingness coefficient_comparison_p Coefficient-test N Coefficient-test missingness
Age 14 MFQ depressive symptoms Motivation/engagement -0.635 0.020 -31.488 < .001 8935 Complete-case analysis < .001 8935 Complete-case analysis
Age 14 MFQ depressive symptoms Executive-control primary proxy -0.012 0.014 -0.888 .375 8935 Complete-case analysis < .001 8935 Complete-case analysis
Age 17 mental wellbeing Motivation/engagement 0.341 0.023 15.069 < .001 8328 Complete-case analysis < .001 8328 Complete-case analysis
Age 17 mental wellbeing Executive-control primary proxy -0.038 0.016 -2.417 .016 8328 Complete-case analysis < .001 8328 Complete-case analysis
Age 23 psychological distress Motivation/engagement -0.212 0.022 -9.607 < .001 7449 Complete-case analysis < .001 7449 Complete-case analysis
Age 23 psychological distress Executive-control primary proxy -0.010 0.015 -0.657 .512 7449 Complete-case analysis < .001 7449 Complete-case analysis
Age 23 anxiety symptoms Motivation/engagement -0.165 0.027 -6.023 < .001 5667 Complete-case analysis < .001 5667 Complete-case analysis
Age 23 anxiety symptoms Executive-control primary proxy -0.021 0.020 -1.070 .285 5667 Complete-case analysis < .001 5667 Complete-case analysis
Age 23 depressive symptoms Motivation/engagement -0.200 0.030 -6.646 < .001 5666 Complete-case analysis < .001 5666 Complete-case analysis
Age 23 depressive symptoms Executive-control primary proxy -0.020 0.021 -0.970 .333 5666 Complete-case analysis < .001 5666 Complete-case analysis
Age 23 loneliness Motivation/engagement -0.166 0.025 -6.675 < .001 7130 Complete-case analysis < .001 7130 Complete-case analysis
Age 23 loneliness Executive-control primary proxy 0.045 0.017 2.747 .006 7130 Complete-case analysis < .001 7130 Complete-case analysis
Age 23 mental wellbeing Motivation/engagement 0.263 0.023 11.305 < .001 7124 Complete-case analysis < .001 7124 Complete-case analysis
Age 23 mental wellbeing Executive-control primary proxy 0.018 0.016 1.133 .258 7124 Complete-case analysis < .001 7124 Complete-case analysis

Across these sensitivity models, the main question is whether the same ordering persists when the later outcome is not just psychological distress, but also wellbeing, anxiety, depression, or loneliness. These analyses are still observational and do not imply that all of these outcomes are interchangeable. Their role is narrower: to test whether the central ordering of predictor families is robust to outcome definition.

In this pass, the sensitivity results remain broadly aligned with the main MCS finding. Lower motivation/engagement at age 14 predicts more age-23 distress, anxiety, depression, and loneliness, and predicts lower wellbeing at ages 17 and 23. The direct coefficient-comparison tests remain statistically clear across these alternative outcomes, which strengthens confidence that the main MCS result is not an artefact of a single distress indicator.

3.8.1 Full model results

For the age-17 and age-23 sensitivity models, predictors and covariates are from age 14 and the survey design is also taken from age 14 because age 14 is the exposure wave. The age-14 MFQ model is different: predictors, covariates, and outcome are all from age 14, and the model omits baseline distress because it is a same-wave sensitivity analysis rather than a prospective one.

3.9 MCS balance-index analyses

This section tests the prediction from Notebook 03 that the balance index \(\psi = \log(\text{distress} / \text{wellbeing})\), constructed from co-occurring age-17 Kessler psychological distress (GDCKESSL) and WEMWBS mental wellbeing (GDWEMWBS) measures, may identify adolescents who are closer to later transition into clinically meaningful psychopathology more efficiently than either component alone. Here that is operationalised as later probable depressive caseness rather than continuous symptom variation.

Three single-predictor survey-weighted logistic svyglm models are compared: distress only, wellbeing only, and the balance index. The retained age-23 outcome is probable depressive case status (HDPHQ2 >= 3). Covariates are age 17 age together with sex, age-14 PTTYPE2 sampling stratum, and age-14 income quintile, and the design uses the age-17 survey weight (GOVWT2) because age 17 is the exposure wave. This is not a strict incident-disorder design, because the same depression screen is not available at age 17; rather, it is a weighted prediction of later probable depressive caseness from the age-17 phase-position proxy.

3.9.1 Descriptive summary

Descriptive statistics for the MCS age-17 balance index psi = log(Kessler / WEMWBS).
n psi_mean psi_sd psi_median psi_q25 psi_q75 kessler_mean wemwbs_mean age23_depression_case_rate
7297 -1.275 0.856 -1.199 -1.796 -0.634 7.698 22.242 0.211

3.9.2 Balance-index model comparison

Survey-weighted MCS logistic balance-index comparison predicting age-23 probable depressive caseness, with AUC, Brier score, and AIC.
outcome model predictor odds_ratio 95% CI (OR) statistic p_value auc brier aic N Missingness
Age 23 probable depressive case (PHQ-2 >= 3) Distress only Age 17 distress 1.790 [1.62, 1.97] 11.721 < .001 0.696 0.154 5138.229 5343 Complete-case analysis
Age 23 probable depressive case (PHQ-2 >= 3) Wellbeing only Age 17 wellbeing 0.635 [0.57, 0.71] -8.335 < .001 0.659 0.159 5281.659 5343 Complete-case analysis
Age 23 probable depressive case (PHQ-2 >= 3) Balance index Age 17 balance index 1.969 [1.74, 2.22] 10.930 < .001 0.696 0.154 5127.060 5343 Complete-case analysis

3.9.3 Fit comparison

Design-based fit comparison across the three single-predictor MCS logistic balance-index models for age-23 probable depressive caseness. AUC indexes discrimination, Brier score indexes overall probabilistic accuracy, and delta AIC is relative to the best-fitting model.
outcome model aic auc brier N Missingness delta_aic
Age 23 probable depressive case (PHQ-2 >= 3) Distress only 5138.23 0.70 0.15 5343 Complete-case analysis 11.17
Age 23 probable depressive case (PHQ-2 >= 3) Wellbeing only 5281.66 0.66 0.16 5343 Complete-case analysis 154.60
Age 23 probable depressive case (PHQ-2 >= 3) Balance index 5127.06 0.70 0.15 5343 Complete-case analysis 0.00

If the balance index shows the best overall mix of discrimination, calibration, and information-criterion fit for this probable depressive-case outcome, that supports the Notebook 03 prediction that the ratio carries transition-relevant information not fully captured by either component alone. The theoretical rationale remains the same: the ratio is not an arbitrary transformation, but the observable that the active-inference framework highlights as the closest empirical proxy to the combined phase-position term.

In the present MCS analysis, the support is clean: the balance index fits age-23 probable depressive caseness best among the three single-predictor models. That is exactly the kind of result Notebook 03 motivates, namely that the ratio can sometimes retain clinically relevant transition signal more efficiently than either component on its own.

3.10 Summary

The MCS analyses ask whether age-14 motivation/engagement or age-14 executive-control is the stronger predictor of later outcomes after adjustment for age-14 distress, age, sex, PTTYPE2 sampling stratum, and income quintile. Using the age-14 survey design, the answer remains clearly in favour of motivation/engagement, and that ordering is supported by a direct coefficient-comparison test. Family conflict, sleep, and peer-related difficulty further shape that pathway, the focused genetic models retain depression and cognition polygenic risk scores as the two targeted exploratory moderators, and the sensitivity analyses extend the same question to separate later symptom and wellbeing outcomes, plus a same-wave age-14 MFQ check. The balance-index analyses additionally test whether \(\psi = \log(\text{Kessler} / \text{WEMWBS})\) at age 17 carries transition-relevant information about later probable depressive caseness (Notebook 03).

4 ABCD Study

4.1 ABCD sample and measurement focus

The ABCD analyses centre on parent-reported CBCL internalising and withdrawn or depressed symptoms as the most defensible longitudinal outcome. Motivation/engagement is indexed primarily by school environment, school involvement, and school disengagement. Executive-control is indexed primarily by the NIH Toolbox Flanker score at ses-02A. For the balance-index analyses (see below), parent-reported CBCL stress (mh_p_cbcl__strs_sum) is paired with youth-reported BAS Drive (mh_y_bisbas__bas__dr_sum) to construct a theory-derived distress-to-motivation ratio.

For sleep, the current analysis uses two repeated and clinically interpretable measures from the Munich Chronotype Questionnaire: sleep duration and social jetlag. They were chosen because they capture two distinct and relevant features of adolescent sleep risk, namely insufficient sleep and circadian misalignment, and because they are available repeatedly in the local extract. The current sleep-risk composite therefore represents shorter sleep together with greater social jetlag.

Only some context variables were estimable in the final ABCD moderation models at the chosen age window. Sleep and peer adversity were the most usable in what follows. (Other local ABCD candidates that could be added in a secondary analysis include family-environment measures, neighbourhood safety or social-context measures, peer victimisation indicators, perceived support or resilience measures, KSADS sleep-symptom variables, and additional linked school or neighbourhood exposures.) The present implementation (as below) prioritised repeated measures with clearer longitudinal alignment and acceptable coverage in the main analytic sessions.

All primary ABCD regression models use ses-02A predictors and ses-05A outcomes. The adjustment set is the same baseline session: ses-02A baseline outcome, age, sex, household income, parent education, area deprivation index percentile, and assessment site.

Demographic and socioeconomic profile of the primary ABCD analytic sample used in the complete-case ses-02A to ses-05A comparison models.
sample characteristic summary
ABCD primary complete-case sample (ses-02A to ses-05A) Cohort members (N) 5,674
ABCD primary complete-case sample (ses-02A to ses-05A) Age at baseline, mean (SD) 11.99 (0.65)
ABCD primary complete-case sample (ses-02A to ses-05A) Female, n (%) 2,992 (52.7%)
ABCD primary complete-case sample (ses-02A to ses-05A) Household income, mean (SD) 7.65 (2.19)
ABCD primary complete-case sample (ses-02A to ses-05A) Parent education, mean (SD) 18.17 (3.06)
ABCD primary complete-case sample (ses-02A to ses-05A) Area deprivation index percentile, mean (SD) 38.89 (25.91)
ABCD primary complete-case sample (ses-02A to ses-05A) Baseline internalising, mean (SD) 47.71 (10.33)
ABCD primary complete-case sample (ses-02A to ses-05A) Race/ethnicity: White, n (%) 3,334 (58.9%)
ABCD primary complete-case sample (ses-02A to ses-05A) Race/ethnicity: Black, n (%) 640 (11.3%)
ABCD primary complete-case sample (ses-02A to ses-05A) Race/ethnicity: Hispanic/Latino, n (%) 1,024 (18.1%)
ABCD primary complete-case sample (ses-02A to ses-05A) Race/ethnicity: Asian, n (%) 114 (2.0%)
ABCD primary complete-case sample (ses-02A to ses-05A) Race/ethnicity: Other/Multiracial, n (%) 546 (9.7%)

4.2 ABCD trajectories

ABCD internalising trajectory through age 15 (ses-05A).

ABCD wave-level descriptive summary for CBCL internalising T-scores through age 15.
wave cohort n_rows n_participants outcome_mean outcome_sd outcome_missing
ses-00A ABCD 11862 11862 48.45 10.64 0.0%
ses-01A ABCD 11201 11201 48.59 10.62 0.0%
ses-02A ABCD 10898 10898 47.70 10.54 0.0%
ses-03A ABCD 10197 10197 47.70 10.61 0.0%
ses-04A ABCD 9496 9496 47.54 10.90 0.0%
ses-05A ABCD 8658 8658 48.40 11.01 0.0%

4.3 Motivation/engagement versus executive-control comparison

This primary ABCD comparison uses ses-02A motivation/engagement and executive-control predictors, ses-05A parent-reported internalising as the outcome, and ses-02A covariates. The covariates entered are ses-02A internalising, age, sex, household income, parent education, area deprivation index percentile, and site.

The fitted model is an ordinary least-squares lm with a standardised ses-05A internalising outcome. All substantive continuous predictors and covariates are z-scored at ses-02A, so B and Std. b are the same for those terms. Site enters as a nominal factor, so its contrast rows are shown on the same standardised outcome scale rather than as a fully standardised beta; the intercept alone remains unstandardised.

ABCD combined-model coefficients for the motivation/engagement and executive-control predictor families, with age-12 predictors and age-15 internalising as the outcome.

ABCD combined lagged model comparing motivation/engagement and executive-control predictors, with model N and missingness handling shown explicitly.
predictor_family estimate std_error statistic p_value N Missingness
Motivation/engagement -0.066 0.013 -4.892 < .001 5674 Complete-case analysis
Executive-control primary proxy -0.031 0.011 -2.924 .003 5674 Complete-case analysis
Direct coefficient-comparison test for the ABCD combined model. Negative values indicate that the motivation/engagement coefficient is more negative than the executive-control coefficient.
outcome estimate_difference std_error statistic p_value N Missingness
Age 15 parent-reported internalising -0.035 0.017 -2.057 .040 5674 Complete-case analysis

In the combined ABCD model, both predictor families contribute, and the motivation/engagement composite has the larger coefficient magnitude. With the expanded standard adjustment in place, the direct coefficient-comparison test is also conventionally significant in this cohort. Therefore, the ABCD cohort also supports the central ordering claim.

4.3.1 Full model results

The full combined ABCD model below includes ses-02A predictors and ses-02A covariates, with ses-05A parent-reported internalising as the outcome.

4.4 ABCD precision-proxy operationalisation sensitivity

The primary ABCD executive-control proxy is the ses-02A NIH Toolbox Flanker score (nc_y_nihtb__flnkr__fullcor_tscore). Two alternative NIH Toolbox indicators with adequate ses-02A coverage were evaluated as operationalisation sensitivities: the Pattern Comparison Processing Speed Task (nc_y_nihtb__pttcp__fullcor_tscore; n ≈ 8,000 valid at ses-02A) and the Crystallized composite score (nc_y_nihtb__comp__cryst__fullcorr_tscore; n ≈ 7,300 valid at ses-02A). Card Sort flexibility and List Sorting working memory, previously used as candidate sensitivities, were discontinued because they had only 5 and 14 valid cases respectively at ses-02A and cannot support a fully adjusted model.

ABCD precision-proxy operationalisation sensitivity checks for the primary ses-02A to ses-05A model. Alternatives are Pattern Comparison Processing Speed and the Crystallized composite, both with adequate ses-02A coverage. Coefficient-comparison p values test whether the motivation/engagement coefficient differs from the alternative precision coefficient within the same combined model.
outcome Alternative precision proxy Field estimate Precision estimate Coefficient-comparison p N Missingness status
Age 15 parent-reported internalising Pattern Comparison Processing Speed -0.063 -0.021 .013 6035 Complete-case analysis Estimated
Age 15 parent-reported internalising NIH Toolbox Crystallized composite -0.058 0.028 < .001 5573 Complete-case analysis Estimated

4.5 Missing-data sensitivity: FIML

As a missing-data sensitivity analysis for the primary ABCD finding, the same combined ses-02A to ses-05A regression was re-estimated using full-information maximum likelihood (FIML) in a saturated observed-variable structural equation model. Continuous predictors were entered on the same standardised scale as in the main model, site was represented with dummy variables, and the fitted covariate set matched the primary complete-case model. This sensitivity analysis addresses missingness without listwise deletion under a MAR assumption.

ABCD missing-data sensitivity analysis for the primary combined model using full-information maximum likelihood (FIML).
predictor_family estimate std_error statistic p_value N Missingness
Motivation/engagement -0.058 0.011 -5.163 < .001 11109 Full-information maximum likelihood (FIML)
Executive-control primary proxy -0.027 0.010 -2.787 .005 11109 Full-information maximum likelihood (FIML)
Direct coefficient-comparison test for the ABCD FIML sensitivity model. The statistic column is a signed z-score (estimate ÷ SE); p is two-tailed.
outcome estimate_difference std_error statistic p_value N Missingness
Age 15 parent-reported internalising -0.031 0 NA .034 11109 Full-information maximum likelihood (FIML)

The coefficient-comparison test under FIML is consistent with the complete-case result, confirming that the ordering is not an artefact of listwise deletion.

4.6 MCS-parity sensitivity: ABCD model without parent education

The standard ABCD adjustment set includes parent education, which has no direct equivalent in the MCS standard covariate set (MCS adjusts for OECD equivalised income quintile, age, sex, and sampling stratum). To verify that the ABCD ordering result does not depend on the additional SES control, the primary complete-case model was re-estimated after dropping parent_education_z. All other covariates — baseline internalising, age, sex, household income, area deprivation index, and assessment site — were retained.

ABCD MCS-parity sensitivity: primary combined model re-estimated without parent education. All other covariates retained.
predictor_family estimate std_error statistic p_value N Missingness
Motivation/engagement -0.065 0.013 -5.057 < .001 6061 Complete-case (no parent education)
Executive-control primary proxy -0.029 0.010 -2.855 .004 6061 Complete-case (no parent education)
Direct coefficient-comparison test for the ABCD MCS-parity sensitivity model (no parent education). Negative values indicate that the motivation/engagement coefficient is more negative than the executive-control coefficient.
outcome estimate_difference std_error statistic p_value N Missingness
Age 15 parent-reported internalising -0.035 0.016 -2.16 .031 6061 Complete-case (no parent education)

Removing parent education from the adjustment set has no meaningful effect on either primary coefficient (changes of < 0.001 in absolute magnitude) and leaves the coefficient-comparison test significant. This confirms that the ABCD ordering result does not depend on the additional SES control with no MCS counterpart. It is also worth noting that parent education was not a significant predictor of later internalising in the primary model after controlling for household income and area deprivation (B = 0.018, SE = 0.012, p = .125), consistent with the negligible perturbation observed here.

4.7 ABCD context moderation

These moderation models retain the same ses-02A predictor wave and the same ses-02A adjustment set as the main ABCD model. Each moderator is therefore evaluated as a baseline contextual influence on the path from ses-02A motivation/engagement or executive-control to ses-05A internalising.

These are ordinary least-squares lm interaction models with the same standardised ses-05A outcome and the same ses-02A z-scored covariate set as the main ABCD analysis.

ABCD context moderation results.
model term estimate std_error statistic p_value N Missingness
Sleep moderation Motivation/engagement composite x sleep-risk composite 0.020 0.009 2.136 .033 5674 Complete-case analysis
Sleep moderation sleep-risk composite x Executive-control primary proxy -0.003 0.008 -0.399 .690 5674 Complete-case analysis
Neighborhood moderation Motivation/engagement composite x area deprivation -0.013 0.013 -0.957 .339 5674 Complete-case analysis
Neighborhood moderation area deprivation x Executive-control primary proxy -0.009 0.011 -0.861 .389 5674 Complete-case analysis
Peer adversity moderation Motivation/engagement composite x peer adversity -0.017 0.049 -0.349 .727 477 Complete-case analysis
Peer adversity moderation peer adversity x Executive-control primary proxy 0.004 0.040 0.101 .920 477 Complete-case analysis

In this pass, the clearest ABCD moderation signal is sleep-related. Sleep is a plausible modulator of how low motivation and disengagement translate into later symptoms, so this is important. In the theory’s language, that makes sleep a candidate field-modulating influence; in ordinary clinical language, it means that impaired physiological regulation may strengthen the pathway from reduced engagement to later distress. Family and neighbourhood context variables are present in the broader extract and remain worth extending, but sleep is the cleanest moderation signal in the current baseline-follow-up window.

4.7.1 Full model results

Each full ABCD moderation model below uses ses-02A predictors, a ses-02A moderator, and the standard ses-02A covariate set, with ses-05A parent-reported internalising as the outcome.

4.8 ABCD sensitivity analyses with youth BPM outcomes

These sensitivity analyses keep the same ses-02A predictors and ses-02A covariates, but replace the parent-reported CBCL outcome with youth-reported Brief Problem Monitor outcomes at ses-05A. Baseline adjustment is outcome-specific: the BPM internalising model adjusts for ses-02A BPM internalising, and the BPM attention model adjusts for ses-02A BPM attention, alongside the same ses-02A age, sex, household income, parent education, area deprivation index percentile, and site covariates.

These are ordinary least-squares lm models on standardised BPM outcomes, with the same standardisation rule as above for the substantive continuous terms.

ABCD sensitivity analyses using youth-reported Brief Problem Monitor internalising and attention outcomes. Coefficient-comparison p values test whether the motivation/engagement coefficient differs from the executive-control coefficient within the same combined model.
outcome predictor_family estimate std_error statistic p_value N Missingness coefficient_comparison_p Coefficient-test N Coefficient-test missingness
Age 15 youth BPM internalising Motivation/engagement -0.075 0.017 -4.502 < .001 5365 Complete-case analysis < .001 5365 Complete-case analysis
Age 15 youth BPM internalising Executive-control primary proxy 0.021 0.013 1.636 .102 5365 Complete-case analysis < .001 5365 Complete-case analysis
Age 15 youth BPM attention Motivation/engagement -0.072 0.017 -4.240 < .001 5098 Complete-case analysis < .001 5098 Complete-case analysis
Age 15 youth BPM attention Executive-control primary proxy 0.014 0.012 1.151 .250 5098 Complete-case analysis < .001 5098 Complete-case analysis

These sensitivity analyses are useful because they test whether the ordering of predictor families is limited to one reporter and one symptom frame. In this pass, the youth BPM internalising outcome shows the same ordering clearly, whereas the youth BPM attention outcome remains more tentative. They therefore complement the parent-report results rather than replacing them.

4.8.1 Full model results

These full sensitivity models use the same ses-02A predictor wave and ses-02A covariates as the main ABCD model, but replace the ses-05A outcome with youth-reported BPM internalising or BPM attention.

4.9 ABCD balance-index analyses

This section tests the same Notebook 03 prediction in the ABCD cohort. Here the balance index is constructed as \(\psi = \log(\text{CBCL stress} / \text{BAS Drive})\), using ses-02A parent-reported CBCL stress (mh_p_cbcl__strs_sum) as the distress-related component and ses-02A youth-reported BAS Drive (mh_y_bisbas__bas__dr_sum) as the motivation-related component. Two binary outcomes are then examined. The first is transition into probable internalising caseness, operationalised as ses-05A CBCL internalising T >= 64 among adolescents below that threshold at ses-02A. The second is youth-reported depression-related functional impairment at ses-04A from the KSADS depression module (mh_y_ksads__dep__impfunct__pres_sx), coded as 1 when impairment is endorsed and 0 otherwise, including the branch-coded non-depressed group.

The pairing of CBCL stress (a parent-reported measure of the child’s distress and stress reactivity) with BAS Drive (a youth-reported measure of approach motivation) is not identical to the MCS pairing of Kessler distress with WEMWBS wellbeing, but it captures the same theoretical structure: the ratio of an avoidance-related observable to an approach-related observable, which the theory identifies as the relevant phase-space coordinate. The fitted models are ordinary logistic glm models with the same ses-02A demographic and socioeconomic covariates as the main ABCD analysis, and both retained models also adjust for baseline internalising severity to keep the comparison anchored to prior symptom burden. The internalising-caseness model additionally restricts the baseline sample to adolescents below the threshold so the focus stays on transition rather than cross-sectional severity alone.

4.9.1 Descriptive summary

Descriptive statistics for the ABCD balance index psi = log(CBCL stress / BAS Drive) at ses-02A, shown separately for the two retained binary outcomes.
outcome n psi_mean psi_sd psi_median psi_q25 psi_q75 stress_mean motiv_mean case_rate
Age 15 probable internalising case (CBCL >= 64; baseline < 64) 4781 -0.351 0.931 -0.405 -1.099 0.288 2.989 4.146 0.092
Age 14 youth KSADS depression-related functional impairment (ses-04A) 5564 -0.203 1.017 -0.223 -0.981 0.511 3.759 4.173 0.224

4.9.2 Balance-index model comparison

ABCD logistic balance-index comparison across the retained binary outcomes, with AUC, Brier score, and AIC.
outcome model predictor odds_ratio 95% CI (OR) statistic p_value auc brier aic N Missingness
Age 15 probable internalising case (CBCL >= 64; baseline < 64) Stress only Baseline stress 1.359 [1.14, 1.62] 3.408 < .001 0.775 0.076 2279.011 4175 Complete-case analysis
Age 15 probable internalising case (CBCL >= 64; baseline < 64) Motivation only Baseline BAS Drive 1.026 [0.91, 1.15] 0.437 .662 0.771 0.077 2290.261 4175 Complete-case analysis
Age 15 probable internalising case (CBCL >= 64; baseline < 64) Balance index Baseline balance index 1.163 [1.02, 1.33] 2.235 .025 0.772 0.076 2285.451 4175 Complete-case analysis
Age 14 youth KSADS depression-related functional impairment (ses-04A) Stress only Baseline stress 1.230 [1.11, 1.37] 3.913 < .001 0.699 0.157 4745.924 4846 Complete-case analysis
Age 14 youth KSADS depression-related functional impairment (ses-04A) Motivation only Baseline BAS Drive 1.006 [0.93, 1.08] 0.170 .865 0.696 0.157 4761.137 4846 Complete-case analysis
Age 14 youth KSADS depression-related functional impairment (ses-04A) Balance index Baseline balance index 1.093 [1.00, 1.19] 2.019 .043 0.697 0.157 4757.087 4846 Complete-case analysis

4.9.3 Fit comparison

ABCD fit comparison across the three single-predictor logistic balance-index models for the retained binary outcomes.
outcome model aic auc brier N Missingness delta_aic
Age 15 probable internalising case (CBCL >= 64; baseline < 64) Stress only 2279.01 0.77 0.08 4175 Complete-case analysis 0.00
Age 15 probable internalising case (CBCL >= 64; baseline < 64) Motivation only 2290.26 0.77 0.08 4175 Complete-case analysis 11.25
Age 15 probable internalising case (CBCL >= 64; baseline < 64) Balance index 2285.45 0.77 0.08 4175 Complete-case analysis 6.44
Age 14 youth KSADS depression-related functional impairment (ses-04A) Stress only 4745.92 0.70 0.16 4846 Complete-case analysis 0.00
Age 14 youth KSADS depression-related functional impairment (ses-04A) Motivation only 4761.14 0.70 0.16 4846 Complete-case analysis 15.21
Age 14 youth KSADS depression-related functional impairment (ses-04A) Balance index 4757.09 0.70 0.16 4846 Complete-case analysis 11.16

In the current ABCD window, the two retained outcomes behave differently, but with a common pattern. For later probable internalising caseness, baseline stress alone still fits slightly better than the balance index. For youth-reported depression-related functional impairment at ses-04A, the balance index is statistically informative and performs better than motivation alone, but stress alone still gives the best overall fit. The ratio therefore does not dominate in ABCD, yet it looks more credible in the depression-focused analysis than when paired with the broader internalising-threshold outcome.

4.10 Summary

The ABCD analyses test whether the same ordering of predictor families appears in an independent cohort with different measures. The main models use ses-02A predictors and covariates with ses-05A outcomes, and with that adjustment set the direct coefficient-comparison test now supports the ordering claim in the main parent-report model. Sleep remains the clearest contextual moderator of that pathway, and the BPM sensitivity analyses provide an additional youth-report check, with clearer support for BPM internalising than for BPM attention. The balance-index analyses test whether \(\psi = \log(\text{CBCL stress} / \text{BAS Drive})\) at ses-02A adds transition-relevant information for both later probable internalising caseness by ses-05A and youth-reported depression-related functional impairment at ses-04A, even though stress alone still fits slightly better in both analyses.

5 Cross-Cohort Synthesis

5.1 Claim-level synthesis

Cross-cohort summary of the main theory-relevant claims.
claim_tested cohort model key_result support_level
Motivation/engagement variables predict later persistence/worsening more strongly than executive-control variables ABCD Lagged linear model, age 12 to age 15 follow-up Motivation/engagement beta = -0.066 (p = 0.000); executive-control beta = -0.031 (p = 0.003); direct coefficient-comparison p = 0.040 supports
Motivation/engagement variables predict later persistence/worsening more strongly than executive-control variables MCS Survey-weighted svyglm, age 14 exposure to age 17 follow-up Motivation/engagement beta = -0.302 (p = 0.000); executive-control beta = 0.021 (p = 0.144); direct coefficient-comparison p = 0.000 supports
Context moderates motivation/engagement and/or executive-control pathways ABCD Lagged interaction models with family/sleep/neighborhood moderators At least one context interaction was estimable in the local ABCD extract partially supports
Context moderates motivation/engagement and/or executive-control pathways MCS Survey-weighted svyglm interaction models with family, sleep, and peer moderators to age 17 Multiple age-14 context interactions were estimable in MCS partially supports
Genetic scores moderate sensitivity to motivation/engagement or context pathways MCS Survey-weighted svyglm interaction models with depression PRS and cognition PGI plus ancestry PCs to age 17 Exploratory genetic-score interactions were estimable with ancestry-PC adjustment, but none reached conventional significance does not support

The strongest cross-cohort pattern is convergent in both cohorts. MCS supports the ordering in which age-14 motivation and engagement are more central than age-14 executive-control for later distress after adjustment for age-14 demographic and socioeconomic covariates, and ABCD now does the same after adjustment for baseline demographic, socioeconomic, neighbourhood, and site covariates in the main parent-report model.

A short interpretive point is important for the balance indices. These ratios should not be treated as universal superior predictors of all later outcomes. The current results are better read as evidence that ratio-type measures may be especially informative when the endpoint is closer to depressive phase-position or depressive impairment, because they compress two opposing components into a single phase-position proxy. Their usefulness should therefore be tested rather than assumed in each cohort.

5.2 Interpretation and limits

This convergence is meaningful, but NOTE THE FOLLOWING!

  • The cohorts do not measure latent active-inference parameters directly.
  • The outcomes are developmental distress proxies, not formal state-transition observables.
  • The motivation/engagement versus executive-control comparison is a theory-guided ordering test, not a claim that one domain matters and the other does not.
  • The MCS balance-index analyses use age-17 predictors for age-23 depressive outcomes, which is a different analytical window from the main age-14-to-age-17 models. The two sets of analyses test different but complementary predictions.
  • The ABCD balance-index analyses pair a parent-reported stress measure with a youth-reported motivation measure — a cross-informant ratio whose psychometric properties are not yet established independently of this study, even though the added depression-impairment outcome is clinically appealing.

5.3 Summary

Across MCS and ABCD, the study provides indirect observational support for the narrower claim that motivation/engagement-related processes may be more central than executive-control-related processes in persistence and recovery-relevant developmental pathways. The models make their adjustment sets explicit and retain the same broad conclusion after standard demographic and socioeconomic covariate adjustment. That support remains strongest and most extensive in the survey-weighted MCS analyses, but it is also statistically supported in the main adjusted ABCD model. The balance-index analyses provide a further, theory-derived test: whether log-ratio measures constructed from distress and wellbeing (or stress and motivation) components carry additional transition-relevant information for depressive-type outcomes beyond either component alone.

Appendix A: STROBE Checklist

Appendix A. STROBE-style checklist mapped onto the present supplement.
Item STROBE_domain What_is_covered_in_this_supplement Location_in_supplement
1 Title and abstract The supplement title identifies the material as cohort-based observational analyses supporting the main study. Title page and overall structure
2 Background and rationale The rationale for indirect developmental tests of the theory is stated explicitly. Research question and inferential scope
3 Objectives Primary and secondary objectives are listed in direct relation to the theoretical claims under evaluation. Hypotheses
4 Study design The supplement describes cohort-specific observational analyses using longitudinal descriptive, lagged, and moderation models. Pipeline overview
5 Setting The cohorts, age windows, and broad data types are summarised. Data sources and analytical logic
6 Participants Wave-specific counts, attrition tables, and sample-flow figures are shown for both cohorts; MCS analyses retain cohort member 1 only, with PRS merges restricted to first cohort members via PNUM=100. Sample flow sections and MCS cohort notes
7 Variables Outcomes, motivation/engagement predictors, executive-control predictors, context variables, genetic scores, and covariates are documented, including the restricted White-European PRS subset in MCS. Variable strategy and operationalisation tables
8 Data sources and measurement Construct derivation choices are described transparently within each cohort. Cohort-specific sections
9 Bias Indirect measurement, missingness, sparse moderators, and genetic-model limits are discussed directly, including restriction of MCS PRS analyses to the White-European CLS PGI subset. Inferential scope and cross-cohort limits
10 Study size Observed analytical sample sizes are reported rather than justified post hoc. Sample flow and cohort sections
11 Quantitative variables Composite construction and main outcome choices are described. Variable strategy and MCS trajectories
12 Statistical methods Trajectory, lagged comparison, context moderation, and genetic moderation models are described. Model comparison framework and cohort analyses
13 Participants flow Retention and wave-level counts are tabulated and plotted. Section 2
14 Descriptive data Wave-level outcome summaries and attrition are presented. Sections 2 to 4
15 Outcome data Outcome trajectory figures and descriptive tables are included for both cohorts. MCS and ABCD trajectory sections
16 Main results The central comparison between motivation/engagement and executive-control predictors is shown with tables and figures. Sections 3 and 4
17 Other analyses Context and genetic moderation analyses are presented separately. Sections 3 and 4
18 Key results Each major section ends with a short summary of what was tested and what was found. Section summaries
19 Limitations Inferential limits and cohort-specific constraints are stated explicitly. Inferential scope and cross-cohort interpretation
20 Interpretation Results are interpreted as indirect evidence about developmental architecture, not direct latent-parameter estimation. Sections 1 and 5
21 Generalisability Cross-cohort convergence and cohort-specific divergence are distinguished. Cross-cohort synthesis
22 Funding and provenance This supplement documents local analytical provenance only and does not restate the primary studies’ funding details. Appendix note
